setting.note: The frame of the hundred tales, set in and just outside Florence in the plague year of 1348. The death-dealing pestilence empties the city; seven young ladies meet by chance in the church of Santa Maria Novella and resolve to flee; with three young men they retire two short miles into the hills toward Fiesole, to a villa traditionally identified with Poggio Gherardo; from there they wander to the secluded Valley of the Ladies; and at the end the company returns to Santa Maria Novella, whence it set out. The hundred tales roam all of Europe, but the setting the map fixes is this Florentine frame, the ground of the greatest book of the Italian Middle Ages.

rights.originalWork: Public domain. Giovanni Boccaccio, written c.1353; the author died in 1375.
rights.translation: English translation by John Payne (1886); he died in 1916, so the translation is public domain in the US and UK/EU.
rights.territoryNotes: Public domain in the US and UK/EU: Boccaccio died 1375, Payne 1916.
rights.attribution: Giovanni Boccaccio, The Decameron (1353), trans. John Payne. Text from Project Gutenberg.

palette.note: The vermeil roses of the gardens the ten flee into, set against the red of the pestilence they leave behind. A deep red that clears WCAG AA on paper.
